Fresh prints overlap a deer trail where the brush grows close on both sides. The pack circles downwind first, testing the party with low growls before rushing from the ferns if no one spots the trap.

Gray silk ties cedar trunks together above a hollow filled with dry bones and shed feathers. The webbing trembles as something climbs silently overhead, waiting for travelers to step into the sticky shade.
A patch of brambles has grown into a cruel knot around an old animal path. When brushed aside, the thorny stalks tear free of the soil and drag themselves forward with a dry, scraping hiss.

Fresh gouges rake the bark from several trees beside a churned-up berry patch. A hungry owlbear crashes through the undergrowth, more interested in driving rivals from its feeding ground than giving chase.
A clever hunting blind overlooks a narrow path where cut branches hide a snare line. The poachers inside panic when discovered, turning a trespass dispute into a sudden volley from cover.
A hollow tree hums with the restless wings of blood-fed stirges. A broken nest spills across the path, and the swarm erupts when light, noise, or warm bodies disturb the rotten trunk.
A brown bear has claimed a slope where ripe berries roll down from storm-broken canes. It rises from the brush with a warning roar, blocking the easiest way through the ravine.

Silk trip lines run between low branches where the trail dips beneath mossy stones. The snares are fresh, and the patient hunter nearby waits for panic to turn a clean path into a web-choked battlefield.
Three shallow cuts on an ash tree point toward a dry ford that does not appear on most maps. Reading the old trail language correctly saves hours; guessing wrong leads into tangled gullies.

A mossy shrine stone lies cracked beneath oak roots, its worn offering bowl filled with rainwater and bright leaves. The place is quiet enough that every snapped twig feels like an answer from the trees.
A carpet of leaves conceals a narrow wash thick with hooked briars. The slope gives way underfoot, turning a simple descent into a painful scramble through thorns and loose stones.
Cold water bubbles from beneath braided roots and carries a faint mineral sweetness. Resting there for a few minutes eases bruises and steadies breathing before the trail climbs again.
A waxed pouch hangs inside a hollow stump beneath a hunter's old trail sign. The owner has not returned in years, and the contents are nearly ruined unless claimed before the next storm.
A harmless-looking patch of leafmold gives way over a waterlogged pocket. Quick movement keeps packs and boots clear; hesitation leaves someone waist-deep in cold mud.
Blue-green fungus glimmers beneath a stand of dead trees, bright enough to guide travel after sunset. Interpreting the glow reveals safe footing, but disturbing it releases choking spores.
A wounded traveler has splinted a broken waymarker and left blood on the bark nearby. Careful field treatment and a quick search can turn the scene from a warning into a useful rescue clue.
Carved posts that once kept predators away from a woodland trail have been snapped and scattered. Replacing them means finding the missing pieces and learning what was strong enough to break them.
A charcoal burner's covered mound still smokes, but its keeper is gone and hurried tracks lead away from the work site. If no one tends the mound, the fire may spread by dusk.
Thin smoke curls from a half-collapsed watchtower that should be empty. Reaching it requires crossing overgrown ground while deciding whether the fire marks shelter, raiders, or a trapped traveler.
Faint pack bells ring from deeper in the forest, always away from the road. Following them may find a lost caravan animal, but ignoring them leaves the sound trailing the party until nightfall.
A cluster of young trees has blackened overnight, and the rot points like fingers toward an older grove. A local woodcutter wants the source found before the sickness reaches good timber.
An ancient ring of standing stones has gone unnaturally quiet; no birds, insects, or wind sounds cross its boundary. Someone has left fresh offerings at the center and vanished afterward.
A black-fletched arrow is buried deep in an oak beside a bloodless cloak. The trail beyond is deliberately confused, suggesting the missing owner wanted pursuit delayed.
A living root bridge over a stream is unraveling strand by strand. Restoring it means finding what is chewing at the roots below before the next group of travelers reaches the crossing.

A night storm has dropped branches across a berry hollow, scattering tracks from deer, wolves, and something heavier. Careful work can recover clean fruit before rain turns the hollow into sucking mud.
Pale mushrooms grow in a perfect circle beneath a lightning-split oak, faintly cool to the touch. The caps bruise easily, and several poisonous lookalikes grow just outside the ring.
A damp hollow shelters useful herbs where water seeps through moss and root. Fresh rooting marks show animals have already churned the ground, so the best plants must be cut cleanly and quickly.
Sharp-scented shoots mark a sunny break in the canopy where loose soil hides edible bulbs. Pulling them carelessly collapses the bank into a badger run and spoils much of the patch.
A fallen trunk buzzes with a wild hive, and broken comb has drawn ants and hungry birds. Smoke, patience, or very steady hands can claim honey before the colony turns defensive.
A ridge of hazel shrubs has dropped a heavy crop onto dry leaves. The nuts are good, but fresh claw marks on nearby trunks suggest the party is not the only one harvesting here.
Two similar plants grow beside an old campsite: one useful for teas, one that causes hours of stomach pain. The campsite notes are rain-smudged, leaving identification to skill and caution.
A shelf of round fungus bursts when disturbed, filling the air with bitter yellow dust. Fast reactions keep the harvest clean; a slow hand turns the patch into a coughing fit.
A fallen pine has made a deep pool where trout hold in the shade. The log is slick and half-rotten, so the best casting spot is also the easiest place to slide into cold water.
A beaver dam has slowed a stream into a clear backwater full of small fish. Harvesting without breaking the dam takes care, because a careless step sends the whole pool rushing downstream.
Sunlight flashes over a shallow run packed with silver minnows. They can be netted for bait or food, but only before passing shadows scatter the school under the roots.
Flat stones along a cold creek hide fat crayfish and sharp little claws. Working the streambed quietly brings a useful catch before silt clouds the water and ruins the chance.
A still pond behind reeds shows bubbles and rings where fish feed below. Choosing the wrong bank means wading into deep muck; reading the water reveals the firm approach.
A heavy snapping turtle has taken a baited line and wedged itself beneath a root. Freeing the hook is possible, but the creature lashes out at anything brought too close.
A spring-fed pool runs clear over white gravel and cold stones. Cleaning wounds there stings sharply, but the water soothes swelling and leaves the party refreshed.
An abandoned wicker fish weir still funnels the stream, though several ribs have snapped. A quick repair can claim the trapped catch before the current tears the structure apart.
Red-brown stone juts from a ferny slope where rain has washed the soil thin. The ore is easy to spot but difficult to free without sending loose rocks tumbling down the hillside.
A rust-colored seep stains the edge of a mossy hollow, leaving nodules of bog iron beneath the water. Each step must be tested before the mud swallows tools or boots.
Tree roots have split a pale quartz vein along a rocky rise. Useful chunks can be pried free, but the same roots make every hammer strike rebound unpredictably.
A stream has cut into a bank of clean gray clay, exposing enough for careful collection. The bank is undercut and will slump if too much is taken from one place.
Leaf litter hides the square mouth of an abandoned prospect pit. The timbers below are rotten, and one bad step breaks through into a bruising fall among damp stones.
Bright flecks flash in the gravel of a narrow creek after heavy rain. Panning the bend takes patience before the water clouds and carries the best sediment away.
A scatter of granite boulders blocks the base of a wooded ridge. Several have clean fractures useful for building stone, but moving them safely requires leverage and planning.
Yellow ochre stains a damp cliff where roots drink from mineral water. The pigment can be scraped into useful lumps, though the wet ledge offers little room for careless footing.
A storm-dropped cedar lies clear of rot, but its crown is tangled in younger trees. Cutting it free without splitting the trunk or dropping branches on the crew takes steady work.
A tall pine offers long, usable lengths of timber if felled carefully between two mossy stones. Misjudging the lean risks wedging the trunk or crushing the best section.
A recently fallen oak remains solid beneath wet leaves, with only the outer bark starting to soften. It is heavy enough that poor cuts waste hours and good wood alike.
A lightning-scarred pine has left resin-rich heartwood exposed near its base. The pieces burn hot and clean, but sticky sap gums tools unless gathered with patience.
A dead limb hangs high above a chopping site, almost invisible among living leaves. The first heavy vibration shakes it loose in a sudden, dangerous fall.
Several mature birches can spare sheets of bark if cut without circling the trunk. A clean harvest yields useful material; a careless one scars living trees for years.
An old coppice has grown straight hazel poles in a forgotten clearing. The shoots are easy to carry, but tangled brambles and hidden nests make the work slower than it first appears.
A mature ash has split down the middle, leaving workable lengths already half separated. The split creaks in the wind, warning that the whole trunk may twist as cuts are made.
